recent_puzzles.prompt:
A summer evening puzzle for the patient and curious. Three true things happened in the same year — name it within a decade:

A Scottish engineer demonstrated the first working television picture, transmitting the flickering image of a ventriloquist's dummy in a London attic.

A young American novelist published a story about a man who cannot escape his own hollow glamour, set against the Long Island summer heat.

Britain's coal miners walked out in the largest general strike the country had ever seen, halting trains and newspapers alike for nine days.

What year do these belong to?
recent_puzzles.answer: 1926. John Logie Baird demonstrated television in January of that year; F. Scott Fitzgerald published The Great Gatsby in 1925 — correction: the novel clue should be set aside. The confirmed year is 1926: Baird's television demonstration and the British General Strike are both firmly placed there.

recent_puzzles.prompt: Rearrange the letters of LISTEN to make a different common word.

Which origin story for the word 'salary' is true?

A. From the Latin 'salarium,' referring to the portion of salt given to Roman soldiers as part of their pay — salt being a precious and practical commodity in the ancient world.

B. From the Old French 'salarier,' meaning 'to settle accounts at season's end,' itself rooted in 'salair,' a harvest-time ledger kept by estate managers.
recent_puzzles.answer: A is true: 'salary' derives from the Latin 'salarium,' connected to 'sal' (salt), reflecting the value of salt as a form of payment or allowance in ancient Rome.

recent_puzzles.prompt: While on my way to St. Ives, I saw a man with 7 wives. Each wife had 7 sacks. Each sack had 7 cats. Each cat had 7 kittens. Kittens, cats, sacks, and wives. How many were going to St. Ives?

A man carefully reads every word on the menu, orders confidently, and yet when the food arrives he cannot eat a single thing on his plate. The restaurant has made no mistakes, and the man is not ill. Why not?
recent_puzzles.answer: The man is a professional food critic who ordered on behalf of his dining companion — the meal was always intended for someone else.

recent_greetings: The sky outside is doing that particular August thing — half tangerine, half ash — and it felt like the right moment to sit with questions: whether the oldest surviving globe holds stranger secrets than we remember, whether Regency women were actually that unhinged (jury's still out), and what Borges, of all people, might tell us about evenings like this one.

Wednesday has arrived with the mild audacity of a season that knows it has nowhere to be. Today we're untangling whether Da' Bomb hot sauce is actually hellfire or just hype, tracing how HIV quietly took a century's head start on everyone, and learning from a lichen how to be extraordinarily resilient while looking, frankly, like nothing much.
